system_description.overview: Datamonk BV is an AI-powered healthcare data migration and quality management platform. It helps healthcare organizations migrate, clean, normalize, and make imaging and related patient data interoperable at scale, supporting standards such as DICOM, HL7, FHIR, LOINC, and SNOMED CT.

products.Datamonk AI-Powered Healthcare Data Plat: End-to-end data acquisition, ingestion, and migration for healthcare systems (PACS, EHR, lab systems, archives). Includes data quality/curation via AI-assisted deduplication and normalization, interoperability via FHIR endpoints, and integrated governance/compliance controls.
products.Data Acquisition & Ingestion: Connects to diverse healthcare source systems; supports one-time migrations and continuous data feeds into cloud archives, VNAs, or research repositories.
products.Data Integration & Interoperability: Maps, transforms, and harmonizes data to DICOM, HL7, FHIR, LOINC, and SNOMED CT standards.
products.Data Quality & Curation: Deduplication, metadata correction, normalization, enrichment via AI-assisted workflows.
products.Data Governance & Compliance: Audit logging, provenance tracking, healthcare privacy/security regulatory compliance.
products.Data Analytics & Utilization Enablement: Produces searchable, research-ready datasets for clinical studies, AI model training, and operational analytics.

infrastructure.cloud_provider: AWS (carve-out — AWS SOC 2 report reviewed annually; physical/environmental controls excluded from Datamonk scope)
regions: Not explicitly named; described as multi-AZ within AWS
infrastructure.physical_presence: No physical offices or servers — fully cloud-based; geographic scope: no physical controls in scope
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (3 AZs: Public AZ1/AZ2/AZ3, Private AZ1/AZ2/AZ3, Protected AZ1/AZ2/AZ3 shown in network diagram; backup process confirms multi-AZ)
additional_cloud: Atlas AWS Account (separate VPC — visible in network diagram; appears to be MongoDB Atlas)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Three-tier subnet layout within a single VPC: Public subnets (NAT GW per AZ, Route Tables), Private subnets (application/compute layer), and Protected subnets (RDS subnet groups). Segmented VPCs with ACLs. Separate Atlas AWS Account VPC for database (likely MongoDB Atlas). IGW for internet-facing traffic.
network_architecture.api_gateway: Application Load Balancer (visible in network diagram, sits in Public AZ2)
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on the application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services; firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management. Firewall access restricted to authorized personnel.
network_architecture.ids_ips: AWS IDS and IPS deployed; intrusion detection system utilized to monitor/detect unauthorized access or anomalies (confirmed in test CC2.1.1 — no exceptions)
network_architecture.vpn: Bastion host with VPN for remote access (mentioned in system description)
network_architecture.tls: TLS using certificates from an established third-party certificate authority
network_architecture.content_filtering: All systems restricted to internet via content filtering system routed through proxy server; email scanning for malicious scripts/viruses
network_architecture.topology: Internet -> Route 53 -> CloudFront -> IGW -> Application Load Balancer (Public subnet) -> Private subnet (compute/app servers via NAT GW) -> Protected subnet (RDS) -> Atlas AWS Account VPC (MongoDB Atlas). Remote admin access via bastion/VPN.

application_architecture.pattern: Not explicitly stated — likely microservices/hybrid given Frontend Team, Backend & Cloud Team, and Migration Experts Team structure, plus separate Atlas account
compute: EC2 or equivalent (described as 'servers hosted'; no ECS/EKS/Lambda mentioned specifically)
application_architecture.api_style: REST/FHIR endpoints mentioned; specific API style not disclosed
application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test environment from production (CC8.1.4 confirmed — changes tested in separate dev/test env before production)

databases: RDS (visible in network diagram as 'Subnet Groups RDS' in Protected subnet)
Atlas/MongoDB (Atlas AWS Account VPC in network diagram)
data_storage.backup_frequency: Daily (CC7.2 control confirmed: 'Backups are taken daily' — no exceptions)

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: AWS IAM / Active Directory mentioned; password policy managed via AWS; no named SSO/IdP product
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for all user accounts with administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1); MFA required for source code tool access (CC8.1.3); MFA required for all remote access (CC6.1.1 test confirmed)
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Periodic forced changes, password expiry and complexity requirements; accounts disabled after limited failed logon attempts; managed via AWS password policy

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used (AWS KMS implied via CC6.1.5: 'uses its cloud provider key management service to encrypt data at rest')
encryption.key_management: AWS KMS (cloud provider KMS; access to production environment keys restricted to authorized individuals)

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Not named explicitly — described as 'source code management tool'; MFA-protected; branch protection rules configured (CC8.1.2: 'merge request requires additional approval' to master branch)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured — every merge request to production/master branch requires additional approval; emergency change process documented
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration management tool used for baseline implementation and rollback (CC7.1.1: 'baseline configurations retained within infrastructure as code; configuration management tool used for implementation and rollback') — tool name not disclosed
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management set up by CTO; monthly patch management reviews; workstations configured for automatic updates and scanned daily for patch compliance; OS patches applied as they become available

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named — described as 'monitoring tool' that monitors system capacity/performance and generates alerts at predefined thresholds (CC5.2.1)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Not named — logging enabled at application and infrastructure level; access to modify logs restricted
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events; tracked in change-management/ticketing application

third_party_services.purpose: Cloud infrastructure — data center, compute, storage, networking, RDS, S3, CloudFront, Route 53, VPC, KMS
Managed database service — separate VPC in AWS visible in network diagram

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and malware protection software installed on all production servers (continuous scanning; AV definitions updated weekly on servers, daily on workstations); installed on Mac workstations and laptops; email scanning enabled
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications (automated tool — not named); internal IT Infrastructure team conducts scans; pre-release vulnerability self-assessment scan also performed
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party (CC4.1.1 confirmed — no exceptions)

untestable: CC2.2.3 — No significant changes to people/roles/responsibilities during engagement period (could not test communication of changes)
CC3.2.1 — No cyber incidents reported during engagement period (cybersecurity insurance response could not be tested)
CC6.5.2 — No customer terminations during engagement period (customer data deletion could not be tested)
CC7.3.1 — No security incidents reported during engagement period (incident response could not be tested)
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — Physical access controls excluded; AWS is responsible for physical security of data centers
A1.2 — Datamonk has no physical office; environmental security managed by building management/AWS
Processing Integrity trust criteria — determined not relevant to the system
Privacy trust criteria — determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: CC7.2 (CC7.4.2): Daily backups, multi-AZ zones configured, alerts for backup failures — confirmed by onscreen inspection
CC8.1.2: Code repository branch rules require approval for every merge to master — confirmed by evidence inspection
CC8.1.3: Source code MFA-protected, time-stamped, author-attributed — confirmed
CC4.1.1: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party confirmed
CC6.2.2: Quarterly access reviews for all user accounts including privileged accounts confirmed
Atlas AWS Account (separate VPC) visible in network diagram — suggests MongoDB Atlas as separate database tenancy

infrastructure.rationale: Multi-AZ (3 AZs) confirmed with daily backups and failover. CloudFront CDN present. AWS carve-out with separate Atlas account shows some architectural maturity. No multi-region. No explicit IaC tool named (described generically). Single cloud provider. Loses points for undisclosed region and no named IaC tooling.

application_architecture.rationale: Separate dev/test environments, branch protection, and code review confirmed. However, no programming languages, frameworks, or specific compute services (ECS/EKS/Lambda) disclosed. No mention of automated testing. Architecture pattern not stated. Significant opacity for a data platform company.

data_layer.rationale: Daily backups confirmed, multi-AZ storage, RDS + MongoDB Atlas visible in diagram, S3 present, data classification and retention policies in place. Per-tenant segregation stated. No caching layer mentioned, no data warehouse disclosed. Healthcare-specific data types (DICOM, FHIR) add context but no encryption-at-field-level details.

security_posture.rationale: Strong: annual external pentest, monthly vuln scans, IDS/IPS, MFA on admin/remote access, quarterly access reviews, cybersecurity insurance, server hardening, AV on all systems, firewall rules reviewed annually. Gaps: no WAF explicitly named, no SIEM named, no specific security tooling vendors disclosed (all generic descriptions).

devops_maturity.rationale: Branch protection and code review enforced, separate environments, emergency change process, IaC described but tool unnamed, automatic patch management. No CI/CD tool named, no container orchestration mentioned, no named source control platform. Loses significant points for opacity on tooling stack.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented, annually reviewed and tested, daily backups, multi-AZ failover, DR plan covers all AZs. No RTO/RPO disclosed. No multi-region failover. DR testing confirmed annually. Section 5 confirms continuity achieved by spinning up servers across all AZs in AWS.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Heavy AWS concentration — compute, storage, CDN, DNS, database (RDS), KMS all on AWS. MongoDB Atlas also on AWS. Only one non-AWS vendor identified (CA for TLS). No meaningful cloud diversification. Entire platform dependent on AWS availability and security posture.

overall.rationale: Datamonk demonstrates a solid compliance foundation for a small early-stage healthcare data company. The network architecture is well-structured (multi-AZ, 3-tier subnets, IDS/IPS, CloudFront), and key security controls (pentest, MFA, quarterly access reviews, daily backups) all passed without exception. Main concerns are: (1) extreme AWS/single-cloud concentration for a healthcare data company, (2) very thin technology disclosure — no languages, frameworks, CI/CD tools, or monitoring products named, suggesting limited maturity or deliberate opacity, (3) 3-month audit window is very short, (4) Processing Integrity and Privacy criteria excluded despite handling sensitive healthcare data.

red_flags: Extremely short 3-month audit period (April 30 – July 30, 2025) — insufficient to assess controls over a full annual cycle; several controls were untestable due to no triggering events
Privacy and Processing Integrity trust criteria explicitly excluded despite handling sensitive patient data (DICOM, FHIR, EHR, lab data) — unusual omission for a healthcare data platform
Entire infrastructure concentrated on a single cloud provider (AWS); MongoDB Atlas also on AWS — no geographic or cloud redundancy
No multi-region failover — an AZ outage may be recoverable but a regional AWS failure would be a single point of failure
No RTO or RPO disclosed in the report
No WAF explicitly named or confirmed in controls
No SIEM named — log aggregation and alerting tools undisclosed
yellow_flags: Technology stack extremely opaque — no programming languages, frameworks, CI/CD tools, container platforms, or monitoring products named anywhere in the 81-page report
Source control platform not named (only described generically as 'source code management tool')
IaC tool described generically ('configuration management tool') — no Terraform/CloudFormation/Pulumi named
No caching layer mentioned (Redis/ElastiCache) for a data platform handling large healthcare datasets
No data warehouse disclosed despite producing 'research-ready datasets for clinical studies and AI model training'
Auditor (Accorp Partners) is a boutique firm — license number PAC-FIRM-LIC-47383 shown; less well-known than Big 4 or established SOC 2 firms
Vulnerability scans performed by internal IT Infrastructure team rather than an independent external tool — potential independence concern
No specific healthcare compliance frameworks mentioned (HIPAA, GDPR) despite handling patient data and being headquartered in Netherlands (GDPR jurisdiction)
green_flags: Clean unqualified opinion — zero exceptions across all tested controls
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party confirmed (no exceptions)
Detailed network diagram present showing 3-tier multi-AZ AWS architecture with separate Atlas account VPC
MFA enforced on all remote access, all admin access, and source code management tool
Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration confirmed by onscreen inspection
Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts
Access revocation within one business day upon termination
BCDR plan documented, annually reviewed, and annually tested
Content filtering via proxy server and email scanning across all endpoints
Per-tenant data segregation stated (system components 'appropriately segmented from other customer accounts' — CC6.1.3)
key_observations: The network diagram on page 19 reveals a separate 'Atlas AWS Account' with its own VPC containing a database icon — this strongly suggests MongoDB Atlas is the primary database, with RDS (likely PostgreSQL) as a secondary or legacy store. This dual-database approach is notable for a healthcare data platform.

Datamonk explicitly excludes Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria, citing them as 'not relevant to the system' — this is a significant decision for a company whose core product is migrating and processing sensitive patient health records. This warrants a direct question to management.

The 3-month audit period is one of the shortest possible for a SOC 2 Type 2 and is a first-time report indicator. Several controls were marked 'could not be tested' due to no triggering events in the short window.

The product handles healthcare interoperability standards (DICOM, HL7, FHIR, LOINC, SNOMED CT) suggesting deep healthcare IT integration, yet no HIPAA, GDPR, or NEN 7510 (Dutch healthcare security standard) compliance is mentioned despite being Netherlands-headquartered.

The organizational structure is small (CEO -> CTO/VP Engineering/CSDO -> Frontend Team/Backend & Cloud Team/Migration Experts Team) suggesting a startup-scale team of likely under 20 people operating a healthcare data platform.

The company uses a separate Atlas AWS Account/VPC for the database layer, which is a good security practice (blast radius reduction) but also means they depend on MongoDB Atlas's own SOC 2 controls for that layer.

Vulnerability scans described as 'performed by the internal IT Infrastructure team' — for a company this size, this may mean manual or semi-automated scanning rather than an enterprise-grade continuous scanning solution.
